title: 10 - Blood Vessels - Flashcards
description: Third-party generated Chapter 10 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck prioritizes the syllabus-core topics of arteriosclerosis, hypertension, atherosclerosis, aneurysms/dissection, and vasculitis, with selected venous and lymphatic concepts for integration.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 10: Blood Vessels
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for topic emphasis, exam framing, and prioritization of Sections 11.1 to 11.5.

source_notes.source_title: Chapter 10 study notes and exam prep questions created from the approved Chapter 10 source set
source_notes.reference_note: Used to compress Chapter 10 into single-fact active-recall prompts.

deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page number from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 10 occupies book pages 361-398.

cards.front: What are the three basic layers of a typical blood vessel wall?
cards.back: Intima, media, and adventitia.

cards.front: What is the main functional role of elastic arteries such as the aorta?
cards.back: They store energy during systole and recoil during diastole to maintain forward blood flow.

cards.front: Which class of vessels is the main site of peripheral resistance regulation?
cards.back: Arterioles.

cards.front: What happens to resistance if the diameter of an arteriole is halved?
cards.back: Resistance increases 16-fold.

cards.front: Why are capillaries ideal for exchange between blood and tissues?
cards.back: They have very thin walls and slow flow across a huge total cross-sectional area.

cards.front: About what fraction of the body's blood is normally contained in the venous system?
cards.back: About two thirds.

cards.front: What are the vasa vasorum?
cards.back: Small vessels that supply the outer wall of large blood vessels.

cards.front: Which gas released by endothelial cells causes vasodilation?
cards.back: Nitric oxide.

cards.front: Which peptide released by endothelial cells causes vasoconstriction?
cards.back: Endothelin.

cards.front: Blood pressure is the product of what two variables?
cards.back: Cardiac output and peripheral vascular resistance.

cards.front: Which hormonal system is central to angiotensin II-mediated blood pressure elevation?
cards.back: The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system.

cards.front: About what proportion of hypertension cases are essential (primary) rather than secondary?
cards.back: About 90% to 95%.

cards.front: Hyaline arteriolosclerosis is classically associated with what settings?
cards.back: Benign hypertension and diabetic microangiopathy.

cards.front: What blood-pressure pattern is most strongly associated with hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis?
cards.back: Severe or malignant hypertension.

cards.front: What additional small-vessel lesion often accompanies malignant hypertension?
cards.back: Necrotizing arteriolitis with fibrinoid change.

cards.front: What does arteriosclerosis literally mean?
cards.back: Arterial wall thickening with loss of elasticity.

cards.front: What is Monckeberg medial sclerosis?
cards.back: Calcification of muscular arteries, usually centered on the internal elastic lamina, typically without luminal compromise.

cards.front: What is fibromuscular intimal hyperplasia?
cards.back: A non-atherosclerotic SMC- and ECM-rich intimal thickening that can follow vascular injury or inflammation.

cards.front: What is an atheroma?
cards.back: An intimal plaque with a fibrous cap over a lipid-rich necrotic core.

cards.front: Which lipoprotein is the principal atherogenic cholesterol carrier?
cards.back: LDL.

cards.front: Which lipoprotein is protective because it removes cholesterol from plaques?
cards.back: HDL.

cards.front: What are the four major modifiable risk factors for atherosclerosis?
cards.back: Hyperlipidemia, hypertension, cigarette smoking, and diabetes mellitus.

cards.front: What is the first pathogenic step in the response-to-injury model of atherosclerosis?
cards.back: Endothelial dysfunction.

cards.front: Where do atherosclerotic plaques preferentially develop within arteries?
cards.back: At branch points, ostia, and other sites of turbulent or disturbed flow.

cards.front: Which cell becomes a foam cell in atherosclerosis?
cards.back: A macrophage that has ingested modified lipids such as oxidized LDL.

cards.front: What innate immune pathway is activated by cholesterol crystals in plaques?
cards.back: The inflammasome, leading to IL-1 release.

cards.front: What is a fatty streak?
cards.back: A flat intimal lesion made mainly of lipid-filled foam cells.

cards.front: Which vascular site is typically the most severely affected by atherosclerosis?
cards.back: The infrarenal abdominal aorta.

cards.front: What are the three principal components of an atherosclerotic plaque?
cards.back: Cells, extracellular matrix, and a lipid-rich necrotic core.

cards.front: What morphologic feature best defines a stable atherosclerotic plaque?
cards.back: A thick collagen-rich fibrous cap with relatively little inflammation.

cards.front: What morphologic feature best defines a vulnerable plaque?
cards.back: A thin fibrous cap over a large lipid core with prominent inflammation.

cards.front: About what degree of coronary luminal narrowing typically causes stable angina?
cards.back: About 70%.

cards.front: What distinguishes a true aneurysm from a false aneurysm?
cards.back: A true aneurysm involves the vessel wall itself, whereas a false aneurysm is a contained extravascular hematoma communicating with the lumen.

cards.front: What is a saccular aneurysm?
cards.back: A discrete focal outpouching of a vessel wall.

cards.front: What is a fusiform aneurysm?
cards.back: A circumferential dilation of a vessel.

cards.front: What are the two most important predisposing conditions for aortic aneurysms?
cards.back: Atherosclerosis and hypertension.

cards.front: How does Marfan syndrome promote aortic aneurysm formation?
cards.back: Defective fibrillin increases TGF-beta bioavailability and weakens the aortic wall through progressive elastic tissue loss.

cards.front: Where do most abdominal aortic aneurysms occur?
cards.back: Between the renal arteries and the aortic bifurcation.

cards.front: What is the approximate annual rupture risk of a 5 to 6 cm abdominal aortic aneurysm?
cards.back: About 11% per year.

cards.front: Which congenital valve abnormality is associated with thoracic aortic aneurysm?
cards.back: Bicuspid aortic valve.

cards.front: What is aortic dissection?
cards.back: Blood entering the media and splitting its laminar planes to create a false channel within the aortic wall.

cards.front: What is the major risk factor for aortic dissection?
cards.back: Hypertension.

cards.front: What feared complication of giant cell arteritis requires urgent treatment?
cards.back: Irreversible blindness from ophthalmic artery involvement.

cards.front: What is the classic alternate name for Takayasu arteritis?
cards.back: Pulseless disease.

cards.front: Which circulation is classically spared in polyarteritis nodosa?
cards.back: The pulmonary circulation.

cards.front: About one third of cases of polyarteritis nodosa are associated with what infection?
cards.back: Chronic hepatitis B infection.

cards.front: What cardiovascular complication makes Kawasaki disease clinically important?
cards.back: Coronary arteritis causing aneurysm, thrombosis, or myocardial infarction.

cards.front: Which ANCA specificity is associated with granulomatosis with polyangiitis?
cards.back: PR3-ANCA.

cards.front: What causes primary Raynaud phenomenon?
cards.back: Exaggerated vasomotor responses to cold or emotion.

cards.front: What is the most important risk factor for deep venous thrombosis of the legs?
cards.back: Prolonged immobilization causing venous stasis.
